title: First Blood
html:
The kappa doesn't move right away.

It stands between the trees watching the group spill out, greenish-grey and roughly the height of a short adult, the machete loose at its side. The shell-disc on its back catches the broken forest light, and the bowl-shaped depression at the crown of its head holds a small puddle of dark water that hasn't spilled yet. It smells like river mud.

Nobody in the group moves either. A couple of people near the front have stopped walking and are just staring, and the ones still filing out of the glass room bump into the people ahead, the whole crowd compressing, most of them not close enough to the front to have seen the machete. The glass room emptied out maybe 30 seconds ago and already the crowd feels smaller than it did inside.

'I've seen worse.' That's a lie.

The kappa is patient. It holds its position and waits, not interested in rushing anything, just watching the crowd with those flat eyes. The machete is steady in its grip, not raised, not dropped, just carried like it's been carried before. Watching it stand there is somehow worse than if it had charged immediately.

"Uncle," Soo-Yun says quietly beside me.

"I see it."

The kappa takes one step forward, deliberate and unhurried, the way something moves when it knows the other thing isn't going anywhere. The machete arm shifts a little as it does.

Someone behind me screams and the crowd breaks. Half the group shoves backward toward the glass room, but the walls have gone solid again and the people pressing against them figure that out fast. The other half scatter sideways into the tree line.

The kappa picks a target: a man near the center of the group, mid-forties, heavyset, still frozen where he stopped. It moves toward him at a short trot and the machete comes up with a Crack! of displaced air.

"Oi!" I shout.

The kappa turns its head. That's all I wanted. The heavyset man scrambles left and gets clear while the kappa is still looking at me.

'Well, now it's looking at me.'

Soo-Yun grabs my sleeve. I don't shake her off.

The kappa is about 3 meters away with a machete longer than my forearm, and everything I have on me is a set of restaurant keys and a pack of candy. The trees are close together on the right side. A branch about shoulder height, dead wood, maybe 2 fingers thick where it meets the trunk. Might snap cleanly. Not much reach, but it's what exists.

"Stay here," I tell Soo-Yun, and cut right without waiting for her to argue.

The kappa tracks me. It angles its path to cut me off, which means it can read movement and adjust, which means running in a straight line is useless. I go for the branch instead. The bark bites into my palm as my hand closes around it and I pull hard, leaning my weight back and using my whole body against the dead wood. It snaps with a dry Crack! that sends a jolt up my arm and rattles my teeth. The branch is about 70 cm, thicker than I wanted at the base, thin enough at the tip to flex.

It's a terrible weapon. I hold it anyway.

The kappa closes the last meter fast and the machete comes down in a wide swing. I get the branch up and the blade connects with a Clang! that jars from my wrists to my shoulder, knocking the branch out of my hands. I step back, catch my heel on a root, and go down hard.

The ground hits my back and drives the air out. Above me the canopy is bright through the gaps between branches. The kappa is already raising the machete for the follow and I can't get my legs under me fast enough.

'That's going to be it, then.'

Something hits the kappa from the side. A rock, palm-sized, connects with its temple with a flat Thwack! and the bowl depression on its head tilts. Water spills from the crown.

The kappa goes down, but it's not finished. It hits the ground rolling, trying to right itself, one arm not responding right, moving slow. I get to my feet in time to see Soo-Yun already loading another rock.

"You threw that?" I ask.

"Kappa weaken when the water spills from their head," she says, eyes on the creature. Her voice is steady. "They regain strength when they refill. We should finish it quickly."

'Where did a 16-year-old learn that.'

The machete is on the ground between us and the kappa. I pick it up. The grip is rougher than I expected, wrapped in something dry and cracked, the blade edge dark with whatever the kappa had instead of blood. The kappa is still moving, one arm dragging, starting to pull itself toward a patch of damp soil near the base of a tree root.

It doesn't make it.

---

Enemy Defeated: Kappa (Tutorial)

EXP Gained: 150

Level Up!

---

The notification appears from nowhere, hanging in my vision like text on a screen. I read it twice before it fades. So it's that kind of situation.

I stand over the kappa. It takes about half a second to confirm that I don't feel anything in particular about what just happened. I keep that and move on.

---

Tutorial System Initialized

Welcome, Participant.

Class assignment pending.

Please proceed to the nearest checkpoint.

---

The second notification fades the same way: just the instruction, no indication of where a checkpoint is or what one looks like. Helpful.

Soo-Yun is at my elbow. She read it too, based on the way her eyes tracked the empty air where it was.

"Does yours look the same as mine?" I ask.

"Level Up as well. EXP the same."

"Class assignment pending?"

"Yes."

Good. Whatever this is, it's applying to everyone equally, at least for now. That's one unknown off the board.

The rest of the group is scattered across the edge of the forest. Most are pressed against the glass wall that won't open or standing in place, eyes moving through the air where their own notifications presumably just appeared. A few are sitting down. The heavyset man I distracted the kappa from is on the ground with both hands over his mouth, looking at the dead kappa from about 4 meters away.

The woman from the restaurant is standing about 6 meters away. She watched the whole fight without moving and she's watching me now.

I hold up the machete. She looks at it, then at me, then back at the forest.

"Are there more?" Soo-Yun asks.

I listen. The canopy above is full of ordinary bird noise, nothing specific from the tree line that suggests movement.

"Probably," I tell her.

"What do we do?"

'Good question.'

The system said checkpoint, which means movement. Standing at the edge of the glass room with a dead kappa and a branch I can't use anymore isn't a plan. Whatever the tutorial wants us to do, it wants us moving.

"We go find the checkpoint," I say. "Stay close, stay quiet, throw rocks at anything that tilts."

Soo-Yun picks up 2 more rocks and puts them in her jacket pocket without commenting.

The man on the ground looks up at me. Mid-forties, pale, shirt damp at the collar, hands pressed flat to the ground like he needed something solid under them through the whole fight.

"Hey," he says. "Can I come with you?"

'More weight, more problems.'

"Can you run?" I ask.

"I'm a gym teacher."

The answer is so specific I almost laugh. "Fine. Get up."

He gets up. His legs are shaking but he manages it in one go. I don't ask his name.

The woman from the restaurant is still there when I turn back around. She's moved a little closer, maybe 2 meters, watching the gym teacher with that same flat expression.

"You too," I tell her.

She looks at me for a moment, something behind her eyes working through it. She pushes off the glass wall and falls in behind the gym teacher without a word.

Soo-Yun checks her rocks. The gym teacher wipes his face with his sleeve. The woman stands just behind him, eyes already fixed on the tree line ahead. Good enough.

The forest runs about 30 meters before the light changes. Something in the canopy breaks ahead, and through the gaps the light catches differently, brighter than it should be for thick forest. Could be a clearing, a checkpoint, more kappas. The four of us are a gym teacher with shaky legs, a 16-year-old with rocks in her pocket, a woman running on empty, and me holding a machete I've never swung at anything before today. There's only one way to find out what's ahead, and standing here isn't it.

I tighten my grip on the machete and walk toward it.
